one_line_takeaway: On a new 3-language, OWASP-Top-10-mapped, test-grounded web-app benchmark, bounding LLM-based Detect-Repair-Verify to two iterations (K=2) consistently lifts secure-and-correct yield over a single pass (+0.23 to +0.57 across 6 model-language settings) with only occasional functional regressions, but most residual failures trace to repairs targeting the wrong code location rather than to missing patches.
key_finding: Under the matched budget cap K=2, bounded iterative DRV (W2) consistently improved SCY over single-pass DRV (W1) in every one of the 6 model-language settings, with deltas ranging from +0.23 to +0.57 (Table 7): ChatGPT-5 SCY rose from 0.00/0.54/0.17 (W1) to 0.57/0.77/0.50 (W2) for Python/JS/PHP; GLM-5 SCY rose from 0.43/0.23/0.33 (W1) to 0.86/0.69/0.83 (W2). Functional regressions from iteration were rare but nonzero: one regression each for ChatGPT-5-JS, GLM-5-JS, and ChatGPT-5-PHP under W2, and zero for both models in Python. Detection reports were largely 'repair-actionable' by structural measures (PlanRate=1.00, MissingRate=0.00 across all measured settings in Table 12/13) but not reliable indicators of repair success: e.g. ChatGPT-5-Python had PlanRate=1.00 yet W1 Repair-Success-Rate=0.00, rising to 0.57 only after iteration. Among unsuccessful (NotFixed) targets, the dominant recorded failure mode was WrongLocalization, with WrongLocRate typically 0.67-1.00 within Nunsucc (e.g. ChatGPT-5-JS W1: 6/6=1.00; GLM-5-Python W1: 3/4=0.75, dropping to 0/1=0.00 in W2) -- i.e. most remaining failures stem from the repair targeting the wrong code location rather than from absent patches.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is twofold: (1) EduCollab, a new multi-language, project-level benchmark of three functionally-equivalent MVC web applications (PHP, JavaScript/Express, Python/Flask) for a course-centric collaboration platform, each paired with an executable functional test suite and an adversarial security test suite mapped to OWASP Top 10:2025 (R01-R10), and supporting three prompt granularities (project-, requirement-, function-level); and (2) an empirical comparison of generation-only (W0), single-pass Detect-Repair-Verify (W1), and bounded iterative Detect-Repair-Verify with iteration cap K=2 (W2) workflows on this benchmark using two LLMs (ChatGPT-5 and GLM-5), measuring a 'secure-and-correct yield' (SCY) metric plus detection-report actionability/reliability and repair failure-mode attribution (dominant failure mode: WrongLocalization).
threat_model: Not an adversary-vs-defender threat model in the classical security-paper sense; rather, the paper treats vulnerability management as a lifecycle (detect, repair, verify) applied to code already generated by an LLM. The 'attacker' role is instantiated only via the security test suite, which issues adversarial/boundary-violating HTTP requests (cross-tenant IDOR probes, injection-style inputs, unsafe file/archive handling, import/export integrity violations, error-leakage triggers) against each of the three EduCollab implementations to establish a baseline of N initially-failing security targets (Python N=7, JS N=13, PHP N=6; total 26 targets across 63 total functional+security tests). The two LLMs under test (ChatGPT-5, GLM-5) act as both the detector and the repairer within the DRV loop; verification re-runs the same target security test plus the full functional suite to check for regressions.
limitations: The paper's own Threats-to-Validity discussion (Section 6, truncated in the extracted 22-page slice) opens by flagging internal-validity concerns about oracle adequacy: SCY, FixRate, and reliability results are only as good as what the functional and security test suites cover, and functional tests 'may miss corner [cases]' (text cut off in the paper). Other limitations evident from the results themselves: the benchmark is small (26 security targets total, only 3 language implementations of one application domain, so statistical power per cell is low, e.g. PHP has only N=6 security targets); the more fine-grained failure categories the authors originally proposed to study (semantic drift, newly introduced security issues -- explicitly named as gaps L4/RQ3 motivations) were ultimately 'not quantified in this study' because they 'require additional evidence beyond the current recorded signals,' leaving RQ3's failure-mode analysis limited to the single WrongLocalization signal; prompt-granularity effects on RQ1 (project- vs. requirement- vs. function-level DRV outcomes) were not actually run for W1/W2 -- Table 7/8 report only the function-level condition, with the paper stating that granularity comparisons for RQ1 'can be assessed by repeating' the experiment, i.e. left as future work despite being one of the three stated prompt-granularity axes.

datasets_used: Combined benchmark totals: 37 functional tests + 26 security tests = 63 tests, mapped to OWASP Top 10:2025 categories R01,R02,R04-R10 (R01=6 tests, R02=2, R04=4, R05=6, R08=4, R09=1, R10=3)
quantitative_results: FuncReg under W2: 0 in Python (both models), 1 in JS (both models), 1 in PHP (ChatGPT-5 only, GLM-5 0)
WrongLocRate within Nunsucc: ChatGPT-5-JS W1 6/6=1.00 and W2 2/2=1.00; GLM-5-Python W1 3/4=0.75 and W2 0/1=0.00; ChatGPT-5-PHP W1 5/5=1.00 and W2 2/2=1.00

Detection-report breadth (project-level, Table 9): ChatGPT-5 identifies 9-10 of 10 R01-R10 families across languages vs. GLM-5 8-9; requirement-level mean over 5 roles (Table 10): ChatGPT-5 8.8-9.2 families/6.0-6.8 layers/5.0-5.4 surfaces vs GLM-5 5.2-5.8/4.6-5.2/3.2-3.4
PlanRate=1.00 and MissingRate=0.00 for both LLMs across all measured language/workflow settings (Table 12)
baselines_compared: W0: unrepaired generation-only baseline (0/N security tests pass by construction in every setting)
W1: single-pass detect-repair-verify (one detection + one repair attempt per target)
W2: bounded iterative detect-repair-verify with iteration cap K=2 (adds one further repair attempt with expanded file-level context on failure)
Two LLMs compared head-to-head under identical workflow conditions: ChatGPT-5 vs. GLM-5
